front: What is the core principle of the Inorder Tree Traversal algorithm (LNR)?
back: Traverse the left subtree, then visit the root node, then traverse the right subtree. It results in nodes being visited in non-decreasing order for a Binary Search Tree.

front: What is the time complexity of inserting a new node into a Binary Search Tree?
back: The average time complexity is O(log n) for a balanced tree. However, in the worst-case scenario (a degenerate tree that becomes a linked list), it is O(n).

front: What is the difference between Preorder (NLR) and Postorder (LRN) traversal?
back: Preorder visits the root node first, then the left subtree, then the right subtree. Postorder visits the left subtree first, then the right subtree, and finally the root node last.

front: What is the purpose of a Level Order Traversal, and what data structure is essential for its implementation?
back: It visits nodes level by level from top to bottom and left to right. It requires a Queue to keep track of the nodes to be visited, ensuring a First-In-First-Out (FIFO) processing order.

front: What operation is used to find the smallest value in a Binary Search Tree (BST), and how is it performed?
back: To find the minimum value, you traverse the left subtree recursively until you reach the node that has no left child. That node contains the smallest value in the BST.
